[[ja:旧慣温存政策]] {{Unreferenced|date=2014年2月28日 (Fri) 01:08 (UTC)|sort key=沖縄きゆうかんおんそんせいさく}} '''Old Custom Preservation Policy''' (旧慣温存政策, きゅうかんおんぞんせいさく), or also known as '''Old Custom Retention Policy''' (旧慣存置政策, きゅうかんぞんちせいさく), was a governance policy implemented in the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}} of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} that lasted from {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) until {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36).
== Overview == === Prehistory and Background === In March {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12), the Ryukyu Disposal Officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之}}, under the orders of Minister of Home Affairs {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, compiled an outline of the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}}. This outline, taking into account the series of samurai rebellions on the mainland—from earlier uprisings to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}—considered Okinawan customs and traditions, particularly the abolition of samurai stipends ({{ill|Abolition of Stipends|ja|秩禄処分}}), the reduction of temple estates, and intervention in forest administration, as developments that would repeat the path from the abolition of feudal domains ({{ill|Abolition of the Han System|ja|廃藩置県}}) to the samurai rebellions in Japan proper<ref group="注">In this article, since the term “{{ill|Japan Proper|ja|内地}}” frequently appears in official records of that era with that meaning, the term “内地” is used in that sense.</ref>, thereby forming the prototype of the Old Custom Preservation Policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=176}}.
After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture through the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and with the former domain king {{ill|Ryukyu Domain|ja|琉球藩|lt=Former Domain|lt_ja=旧藩}} King {{ill|King Shōtai|ja|尚泰王|lt=Shōtai|lt_ja=尚泰}} being taken to Tokyo, the commoners—who had long suffered under the fiscal distress of the Ryukyu royal government and its harsh tribute demands since the early 19th century near the end of the Edo period—even voiced support for rule by Japan proper{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}.
=== Emergence of the Policy === ==== Immediately after the Ryukyu Disposal ==== After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, despite advocating for moderate reforms as noted above, plans were laid to implement ordinances on par with those in Japan proper by {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} (Meiji 18).
However, as soon as King Shōtai’s relocation to Tokyo was decided, the former {{ill|Sansikan|ja|三司官}} leader {{ill|Kamegawa Moritake Oyakata|ja|亀川盛武親方}}, at the forefront of what became known as the {{ill|Kamegawa Party|ja|亀川党}},<ref group="注">Later, this group organized into the Stubborn Party and led the defection from Qing. Kamegawa was a leader of the reactionary forces.</ref> and figures such as the {{ill|Kume Samurai|ja|久米士族}}, began secret resistance by seeking aid from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}}, among other measures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}. The disposal officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之|lt=Matsuda|lt_ja=松田}} gathered the former domain samurai and pressed for reforms of the old system, but the unity of the old samurai was firmly unattainable{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181-182}}. On May 18 of {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}}, the first Okinawa Prefectural Governor {{ill|Nabeshima Naoki|ja|鍋島直彬}} took office. Coincidentally, due to protests from the Qing Empire, negotiations concerning Ryukyu had commenced, and there was serious concern that Okinawa’s unrest would adversely affect these diplomatic talks—necessitating strict avoidance. Ultimately, Nabeshima had no choice but to adopt Matsuda’s moderate reform approach{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=182-183}}. The former Shuri samurai resisted by submitting haphazard documents to the prefectural office, and although Nabeshima and his colleagues attempted to persuade local district officials, these officials, fearing reprisals from the Shuri samurai, evaded engagement—resulting in repeated failures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
Thus, the discontent among the samurai class, who had opposed the Ryukyu Disposal even before being stripped of their privileges (such as through the abolition of stipends), much like their disaffected counterparts on the mainland, grew significantly. This discontent later manifested in concrete actions in July, such as the {{ill|Sanshi Incident|ja|サンシー事件}} and the “Former Domain Subjects’ Blood Pledge Document”<ref group="注">Officials of the samurai class in various regions gathered residents at the sacred site {{ill|Sacred Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)|lt=Utaki|lt_ja=御嶽}} and had them pledge loyalty to the Ryukyu Domain King before the gods, as evidenced by the {{ill|Blood Pledge Document|ja|血判状}}.</ref>. However, once these incidents came to light, the tide turned<ref group="注">In other words, evidence of resistance or rebellion was obtained.</ref>, prompting forceful intervention by Okinawa Prefectural Police officials. Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881年}} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892年}} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

[[ja:旧慣温存政策]] {{Unreferenced|date=2014年2月28日 (Fri) 01:08 (UTC)|sort key=沖縄きゆうかんおんそんせいさく}} '''Old Custom Preservation Policy''' (旧慣温存政策, きゅうかんおんぞんせいさく), or also known as '''Old Custom Retention Policy''' (旧慣存置政策, きゅうかんぞんちせいさく), was a governance policy implemented in the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}} of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} that lasted from {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) until {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36).
== Overview == === Prehistory and Background === In March {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12), the Ryukyu Disposal Officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之}}, under the orders of Minister of Home Affairs {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, compiled an outline of the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}}. This outline, taking into account the series of samurai rebellions on the mainland—from earlier uprisings to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}—considered Okinawan customs and traditions, particularly the abolition of samurai stipends ({{ill|Abolition of Stipends|ja|秩禄処分}}), the reduction of temple estates, and intervention in forest administration, as developments that would repeat the path from the abolition of feudal domains ({{ill|Abolition of the Han System|ja|廃藩置県}}) to the samurai rebellions in Japan proper<ref group="注">In this article, since the term “{{ill|Japan Proper|ja|内地}}” frequently appears in official records of that era with that meaning, the term “内地” is used in that sense.</ref>, thereby forming the prototype of the Old Custom Preservation Policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=176}}.
After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture through the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and with the former domain king {{ill|Ryukyu Domain|ja|琉球藩|lt=Former Domain|lt_ja=旧藩}} King {{ill|King Shōtai|ja|尚泰王|lt=Shōtai|lt_ja=尚泰}} being taken to Tokyo, the commoners—who had long suffered under the fiscal distress of the Ryukyu royal government and its harsh tribute demands since the early 19th century near the end of the Edo period—even voiced support for rule by Japan proper{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}.
=== Emergence of the Policy === ==== Immediately after the Ryukyu Disposal ==== After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, despite advocating for moderate reforms as noted above, plans were laid to implement ordinances on par with those in Japan proper by {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} (Meiji 18).
However, as soon as King Shōtai’s relocation to Tokyo was decided, the former {{ill|Sansikan|ja|三司官}} leader {{ill|Kamegawa Moritake Oyakata|ja|亀川盛武親方}}, at the forefront of what became known as the {{ill|Kamegawa Party|ja|亀川党}},<ref group="注">Later, this group organized into the Stubborn Party and led the defection from Qing. Kamegawa was a leader of the reactionary forces.</ref> and figures such as the {{ill|Kume Samurai|ja|久米士族}}, began secret resistance by seeking aid from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}}, among other measures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}. The disposal officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之|lt=Matsuda|lt_ja=松田}} gathered the former domain samurai and pressed for reforms of the old system, but the unity of the old samurai was firmly unattainable{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181-182}}. On May 18 of {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}}, the first Okinawa Prefectural Governor {{ill|Nabeshima Naoki|ja|鍋島直彬}} took office. Coincidentally, due to protests from the Qing Empire, negotiations concerning Ryukyu had commenced, and there was serious concern that Okinawa’s unrest would adversely affect these diplomatic talks—necessitating strict avoidance. Ultimately, Nabeshima had no choice but to adopt Matsuda’s moderate reform approach{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=182-183}}. The former Shuri samurai resisted by submitting haphazard documents to the prefectural office, and although Nabeshima and his colleagues attempted to persuade local district officials, these officials, fearing reprisals from the Shuri samurai, evaded engagement—resulting in repeated failures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
Thus, the discontent among the samurai class, who had opposed the Ryukyu Disposal even before being stripped of their privileges (such as through the abolition of stipends), much like their disaffected counterparts on the mainland, grew significantly. This discontent later manifested in concrete actions in July, such as the {{ill|Sanshi Incident|ja|サンシー事件}} and the “Former Domain Subjects’ Blood Pledge Document”<ref group="注">Officials of the samurai class in various regions gathered residents at the sacred site {{ill|Sacred Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)|lt=Utaki|lt_ja=御嶽}} and had them pledge loyalty to the Ryukyu Domain King before the gods, as evidenced by the {{ill|Blood Pledge Document|ja|血判状}}.</ref>. However, once these incidents came to light, the tide turned<ref group="注">In other words, evidence of resistance or rebellion was obtained.</ref>, prompting forceful intervention by Okinawa Prefectural Police officials. Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881年}} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892年}} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

[[ja:旧慣温存政策]] {{Unreferenced|date=2014年2月28日 (Fri) 01:08 (UTC)|sort key=沖縄きゆうかんおんそんせいさく}} '''Old Custom Preservation Policy''' (旧慣温存政策, きゅうかんおんぞんせいさく), or also known as '''Old Custom Retention Policy''' (旧慣存置政策, きゅうかんぞんちせいさく), was a governance policy implemented in the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}} of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} that lasted from {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) until {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36).
== Overview == === Prehistory and Background === In March {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12), the Ryukyu Disposal Officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之}}, under the orders of Minister of Home Affairs {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, compiled an outline of the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}}. This outline, taking into account the series of samurai rebellions on the mainland—from earlier uprisings to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}—considered Okinawan customs and traditions, particularly the abolition of samurai stipends ({{ill|Abolition of Stipends|ja|秩禄処分}}), the reduction of temple estates, and intervention in forest administration, as developments that would repeat the path from the abolition of feudal domains ({{ill|Abolition of the Han System|ja|廃藩置県}}) to the samurai rebellions in Japan proper<ref group="注">In this article, since the term “{{ill|Japan Proper|ja|内地}}” frequently appears in official records of that era with that meaning, the term “内地” is used in that sense.</ref>, thereby forming the prototype of the Old Custom Preservation Policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=176}}.
After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture through the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and with the former domain king {{ill|Ryukyu Domain|ja|琉球藩|lt=Former Domain|lt_ja=旧藩}} King {{ill|King Shōtai|ja|尚泰王|lt=Shōtai|lt_ja=尚泰}} being taken to Tokyo, the commoners—who had long suffered under the fiscal distress of the Ryukyu royal government and its harsh tribute demands since the early 19th century near the end of the Edo period—even voiced support for rule by Japan proper{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}.
=== Emergence of the Policy === ==== Immediately after the Ryukyu Disposal ==== After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, despite advocating for moderate reforms as noted above, plans were laid to implement ordinances on par with those in Japan proper by {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} (Meiji 18).
However, as soon as King Shōtai’s relocation to Tokyo was decided, the former {{ill|Sansikan|ja|三司官}} leader {{ill|Kamegawa Moritake Oyakata|ja|亀川盛武親方}}, at the forefront of what became known as the {{ill|Kamegawa Party|ja|亀川党}},<ref group="注">Later, this group organized into the Stubborn Party and led the defection from Qing. Kamegawa was a leader of the reactionary forces.</ref> and figures such as the {{ill|Kume Samurai|ja|久米士族}}, began secret resistance by seeking aid from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}}, among other measures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}. The disposal officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之|lt=Matsuda|lt_ja=松田}} gathered the former domain samurai and pressed for reforms of the old system, but the unity of the old samurai was firmly unattainable{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181-182}}. On May 18 of {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}}, the first Okinawa Prefectural Governor {{ill|Nabeshima Naoki|ja|鍋島直彬}} took office. Coincidentally, due to protests from the Qing Empire, negotiations concerning Ryukyu had commenced, and there was serious concern that Okinawa’s unrest would adversely affect these diplomatic talks—necessitating strict avoidance. Ultimately, Nabeshima had no choice but to adopt Matsuda’s moderate reform approach{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=182-183}}. The former Shuri samurai resisted by submitting haphazard documents to the prefectural office, and although Nabeshima and his colleagues attempted to persuade local district officials, these officials, fearing reprisals from the Shuri samurai, evaded engagement—resulting in repeated failures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
Thus, the discontent among the samurai class, who had opposed the Ryukyu Disposal even before being stripped of their privileges (such as through the abolition of stipends), much like their disaffected counterparts on the mainland, grew significantly. This discontent later manifested in concrete actions in July, such as the {{ill|Sanshi Incident|ja|サンシー事件}} and the “Former Domain Subjects’ Blood Pledge Document”<ref group="注">Officials of the samurai class in various regions gathered residents at the sacred site {{ill|Sacred Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)|lt=Utaki|lt_ja=御嶽}} and had them pledge loyalty to the Ryukyu Domain King before the gods, as evidenced by the {{ill|Blood Pledge Document|ja|血判状}}.</ref>. However, once these incidents came to light, the tide turned<ref group="注">In other words, evidence of resistance or rebellion was obtained.</ref>, prompting forceful intervention by Okinawa Prefectural Police officials. Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881st year }} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892nd year }} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903年}} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881st year }} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892nd year }} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903rd year }} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

[[ja:旧慣温存政策]] {{Unreferenced|date=2014th year 2月28日 (Fri) 01:08 (UTC)|sort key=沖縄きゆうかんおんそんせいさく}} '''Old Custom Preservation Policy''' (旧慣温存政策, きゅうかんおんぞんせいさく), or also known as '''Old Custom Retention Policy''' (旧慣存置政策, きゅうかんぞんちせいさく), was a governance policy implemented in the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}} of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} that lasted from {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) until {{ill|1903|ja|1903rd year }} (Meiji 36).
== Overview == === Prehistory and Background === In March {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12), the Ryukyu Disposal Officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之}}, under the orders of Minister of Home Affairs {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, compiled an outline of the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}}. This outline, taking into account the series of samurai rebellions on the mainland—from earlier uprisings to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}—considered Okinawan customs and traditions, particularly the abolition of samurai stipends ({{ill|Abolition of Stipends|ja|秩禄処分}}), the reduction of temple estates, and intervention in forest administration, as developments that would repeat the path from the abolition of feudal domains ({{ill|Abolition of the Han System|ja|廃藩置県}}) to the samurai rebellions in Japan proper<ref group="注">In this article, since the term “{{ill|Japan Proper|ja|内地}}” frequently appears in official records of that era with that meaning, the term “内地” is used in that sense.</ref>, thereby forming the prototype of the Old Custom Preservation Policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=176}}.
After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture through the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and with the former domain king {{ill|Ryukyu Domain|ja|琉球藩|lt=Former Domain|lt_ja=旧藩}} King {{ill|King Shōtai|ja|尚泰王|lt=Shōtai|lt_ja=尚泰}} being taken to Tokyo, the commoners—who had long suffered under the fiscal distress of the Ryukyu royal government and its harsh tribute demands since the early 19th century near the end of the Edo period—even voiced support for rule by Japan proper{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}.
=== Emergence of the Policy === ==== Immediately after the Ryukyu Disposal ==== After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, despite advocating for moderate reforms as noted above, plans were laid to implement ordinances on par with those in Japan proper by {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} (Meiji 18).
However, as soon as King Shōtai’s relocation to Tokyo was decided, the former {{ill|Sansikan|ja|三司官}} leader {{ill|Kamegawa Moritake Oyakata|ja|亀川盛武親方}}, at the forefront of what became known as the {{ill|Kamegawa Party|ja|亀川党}},<ref group="注">Later, this group organized into the Stubborn Party and led the defection from Qing. Kamegawa was a leader of the reactionary forces.</ref> and figures such as the {{ill|Kume Samurai|ja|久米士族}}, began secret resistance by seeking aid from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}}, among other measures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}. The disposal officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之|lt=Matsuda|lt_ja=松田}} gathered the former domain samurai and pressed for reforms of the old system, but the unity of the old samurai was firmly unattainable{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181-182}}. On May 18 of {{ill|1879|ja|1879年}}, the first Okinawa Prefectural Governor {{ill|Nabeshima Naoki|ja|鍋島直彬}} took office. Coincidentally, due to protests from the Qing Empire, negotiations concerning Ryukyu had commenced, and there was serious concern that Okinawa’s unrest would adversely affect these diplomatic talks—necessitating strict avoidance. Ultimately, Nabeshima had no choice but to adopt Matsuda’s moderate reform approach{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=182-183}}. The former Shuri samurai resisted by submitting haphazard documents to the prefectural office, and although Nabeshima and his colleagues attempted to persuade local district officials, these officials, fearing reprisals from the Shuri samurai, evaded engagement—resulting in repeated failures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
Thus, the discontent among the samurai class, who had opposed the Ryukyu Disposal even before being stripped of their privileges (such as through the abolition of stipends), much like their disaffected counterparts on the mainland, grew significantly. This discontent later manifested in concrete actions in July, such as the {{ill|Sanshi Incident|ja|サンシー事件}} and the “Former Domain Subjects’ Blood Pledge Document”<ref group="注">Officials of the samurai class in various regions gathered residents at the sacred site {{ill|Sacred Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)|lt=Utaki|lt_ja=御嶽}} and had them pledge loyalty to the Ryukyu Domain King before the gods, as evidenced by the {{ill|Blood Pledge Document|ja|血判状}}.</ref>. However, once these incidents came to light, the tide turned<ref group="注">In other words, evidence of resistance or rebellion was obtained.</ref>, prompting forceful intervention by Okinawa Prefectural Police officials. Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881st year }} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892nd year }} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903rd year }} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881st year }} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892nd year }} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.
=== Impact of the Policy === It is believed that the major political upheaval during the {{ill|Political Change of Meiji 14|ja|明治十四年の政変}} significantly shook the ruling foundation, leading the new government and prefectural governors to favor maintaining the status quo in order to suppress discontent among both the royal family and the samurai. Thus, even after the Ryukyu Disposal and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the tax system and local administrative structures inherited from the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|ja|琉球王国}} continued. The {{ill|Royal Family|ja|王族}}, in principle, relocated to Tokyo and were incorporated into the nobility (kazoku). This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family). The family stipends ({{ill|Family Stipend|ja|家禄}}) and fiefs of the higher-ranking samurai were provisionally secured, while the lower-ranking, stipend-less samurai lost their positions; furthermore, the traditional land allocation system—which did not permit land ownership by {{ill|Farmers|ja|農民}}—and the in-kind tax system based on it continued. In addition, local samurai officials enjoyed tax exemption privileges, and the head tax on the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} ({{ill|Head Tax|ja|人頭税}}) was likewise preserved. The most pressing concern—the issue of Ryukyu’s affiliation—was resolved by Japan’s victory in the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}}, which also led to the disappearance of the pro-Qing faction in public sentiment.
=== End of the Policy === After witnessing the dire plight of commoners and the tyranny of the samurai on {{ill|Miyakojima|ja|宮古島}}, figures such as {{ill|Nakamura Jūsaku|ja|中村十作}} appealed to bodies like the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会}}, which in turn swayed public opinion toward the abolition of the old customs. Consequently, beginning in {{ill|1899|ja|1899年}} (Meiji 32), the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}} was enacted, land tax reform similar to that in Japan proper commenced, and in {{ill|1903|ja|1903rd year }} (Meiji 36) the land readjustment projects were completed and the head tax on the Sakishima Islands abolished—thus bringing an end to the Old Custom Preservation Policy. However, monetary stipends for the compensated samurai continued until {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).

[[ja:旧慣温存政策]] {{Unreferenced|date=2014th year 2月28日 (Fri) 01:08 (UTC)|sort key=沖縄きゆうかんおんそんせいさく}} '''Old Custom Preservation Policy''' (旧慣温存政策, きゅうかんおんぞんせいさく), or also known as '''Old Custom Retention Policy''' (旧慣存置政策, きゅうかんぞんちせいさく), was a governance policy implemented in the {{ill|Meiji Period|ja|明治}} of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} that lasted from {{ill|1879|ja|1879th year }} (Meiji 12) until {{ill|1903|ja|1903rd year }} (Meiji 36).
== Overview == === Prehistory and Background === In March {{ill|1879|ja|1879th year }} (Meiji 12), the Ryukyu Disposal Officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之}}, under the orders of Minister of Home Affairs {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}}, compiled an outline of the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}}. This outline, taking into account the series of samurai rebellions on the mainland—from earlier uprisings to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|ja|西南戦争}}—considered Okinawan customs and traditions, particularly the abolition of samurai stipends ({{ill|Abolition of Stipends|ja|秩禄処分}}), the reduction of temple estates, and intervention in forest administration, as developments that would repeat the path from the abolition of feudal domains ({{ill|Abolition of the Han System|ja|廃藩置県}}) to the samurai rebellions in Japan proper<ref group="注">In this article, since the term “{{ill|Japan Proper|ja|内地}}” frequently appears in official records of that era with that meaning, the term “内地” is used in that sense.</ref>, thereby forming the prototype of the Old Custom Preservation Policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=176}}.
After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture through the {{ill|Okinawa History#Ryukyu Disposal|ja|沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|lt=Ryukyu Disposal|lt_ja=琉球処分}} in 1879 (Meiji 12), and with the former domain king {{ill|Ryukyu Domain|ja|琉球藩|lt=Former Domain|lt_ja=旧藩}} King {{ill|King Shōtai|ja|尚泰王|lt=Shōtai|lt_ja=尚泰}} being taken to Tokyo, the commoners—who had long suffered under the fiscal distress of the Ryukyu royal government and its harsh tribute demands since the early 19th century near the end of the Edo period—even voiced support for rule by Japan proper{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}.
=== Emergence of the Policy === ==== Immediately after the Ryukyu Disposal ==== After the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, despite advocating for moderate reforms as noted above, plans were laid to implement ordinances on par with those in Japan proper by {{ill|1885|ja|1885th year }} (Meiji 18).
However, as soon as King Shōtai’s relocation to Tokyo was decided, the former {{ill|Sansikan|ja|三司官}} leader {{ill|Kamegawa Moritake Oyakata|ja|亀川盛武親方}}, at the forefront of what became known as the {{ill|Kamegawa Party|ja|亀川党}},<ref group="注">Later, this group organized into the Stubborn Party and led the defection from Qing. Kamegawa was a leader of the reactionary forces.</ref> and figures such as the {{ill|Kume Samurai|ja|久米士族}}, began secret resistance by seeking aid from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}}, among other measures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181}}. The disposal officer {{ill|Matsuda Michiyuki|ja|松田道之|lt=Matsuda|lt_ja=松田}} gathered the former domain samurai and pressed for reforms of the old system, but the unity of the old samurai was firmly unattainable{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=181-182}}. On May 18 of {{ill|1879|ja|1879th year }}, the first Okinawa Prefectural Governor {{ill|Nabeshima Naoki|ja|鍋島直彬}} took office. Coincidentally, due to protests from the Qing Empire, negotiations concerning Ryukyu had commenced, and there was serious concern that Okinawa’s unrest would adversely affect these diplomatic talks—necessitating strict avoidance. Ultimately, Nabeshima had no choice but to adopt Matsuda’s moderate reform approach{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=182-183}}. The former Shuri samurai resisted by submitting haphazard documents to the prefectural office, and although Nabeshima and his colleagues attempted to persuade local district officials, these officials, fearing reprisals from the Shuri samurai, evaded engagement—resulting in repeated failures{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
Thus, the discontent among the samurai class, who had opposed the Ryukyu Disposal even before being stripped of their privileges (such as through the abolition of stipends), much like their disaffected counterparts on the mainland, grew significantly. This discontent later manifested in concrete actions in July, such as the {{ill|Sanshi Incident|ja|サンシー事件}} and the “Former Domain Subjects’ Blood Pledge Document”<ref group="注">Officials of the samurai class in various regions gathered residents at the sacred site {{ill|Sacred Utaki|ja|御嶽 (沖縄)|lt=Utaki|lt_ja=御嶽}} and had them pledge loyalty to the Ryukyu Domain King before the gods, as evidenced by the {{ill|Blood Pledge Document|ja|血判状}}.</ref>. However, once these incidents came to light, the tide turned<ref group="注">In other words, evidence of resistance or rebellion was obtained.</ref>, prompting forceful intervention by Okinawa Prefectural Police officials. Moreover, as part of the opposition to tribute payments to the prefectural office, tribute rice that had been secretly transported from {{ill|Miyako Islands|ja|宮古島}} and {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|ja|八重山}} to Naha was confiscated by these officials{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}. The prefectural office acted swiftly, and by the end of July, the establishment of the main police station, along with the Naha and Shuri branches, was promptly decided{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=183}}.
==== Nabeshima Administration: Preservation of the Old Stipend Order as an Aspect of Old Custom Preservation alongside the Construction of the Prefectural Government System ==== From September onward, the reorganization of a new administrative structure under the prefectural office accelerated. The Okinawa Prefectural Government established its main police headquarters, as well as branch offices in Kumejima, Miyakojima, Yaeyama, and Haji. In October, a Shuri Office was set up. Meanwhile, local guard posts and storage facilities on the islands were temporarily left unchanged as part of the old custom preservation policy{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=187-188}}.
Regarding the stipend order, it was determined that among the higher-ranking samurai, those with notable merit would receive both family stipends and fiefs, while those with lesser merit would receive only fiefs. Thus, the old feudal system—providing land and collecting tribute—was maintained (although hereditary stipends were not granted), and by 1879 (Meiji 12) the stipend policy had been established{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=188}}. Furthermore, with the dissolution of the royal household order, even the registration of family registers for the samurai subject to the stipend system became extremely challenging{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=195}}<ref group="注">Note: At that time, farmers did not have surnames recorded in official documents and remained bound by the feudal system. Although the timing differed, this was similarly the case in Japan proper.</ref>.
Meanwhile, the old stipend-less samurai, led by {{ill|Chikutono|ja|筑登之}}, lost their positions with the collapse of the royal household and its order, and the issue of allowances became a challenge for both the new government and the prefectural office, forcing them to take up unfamiliar trades or agriculture to make ends meet{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194}}. Around this time, the Minister of Home Affairs had been replaced by {{ill|Matsukata Masayoshi|ja|松方正義}}, and Nabeshima, acting in his capacity as prefectural governor, strongly petitioned the central government to allocate a budget for providing endowment money to the old samurai—especially those stipend-less ones who, despite outward obedience, had no means of livelihood. The government, also under severe financial strain, experienced prolonged disputes and delays. In the end, endowment money and similar allowances were disbursed to the stipend-less or inadequately compensated samurai only after {{ill|1881|ja|1881st year }} (Meiji 14), when Matsukata became Minister of Finance and Nabeshima was dismissed{{sfn|後藤新|2016|p=194-197}}.
==== Uesugi Administration: Status Quo ==== The second prefectural governor, {{ill|Prefectural Governor|ja|県令}}, {{ill|Uesugi Shigenori|ja|上杉茂憲}}, personally inspected the prefecture and was deeply moved by the hardships of the {{ill|Commoners|ja|庶民}} and the negligence of local officials; he then traveled to Tokyo to submit a reform proposal. His enthusiasm moved high-ranking government officials, and {{ill|Ozaki Sanryō|ja|尾崎三良|lt=Sanrō|lt_ja=三良}} was dispatched to inspect the situation. However, upon his return, Ozaki reported that "the governors’ indiscriminate attempts to change the old customs had harmed public sentiment."<ref group="注">In Ozaki’s autobiography, he described Uesugi as "an out-of-touch aristocrat who rose by inheritance."</ref> In order to restore order, {{ill|Iwamura Michitoshi|ja|岩村通俊}} was dispatched, and Uesugi was reassigned as a Councillor in the House of Peers, thereby resolving the issue. The reform proposal was rejected, and avoiding further provocation of the samurai class was given priority.
==== Narahara Administration: Suppression in Exchange for Preserving Old Customs ==== In {{ill|1892|ja|1892nd year }} (Meiji 25), the forceful, long-term prefectural administration of {{ill|Narahara Shigeru|ja|奈良原繁}} began, and it clashed with figures such as {{ill|Sha Kasei|ja|謝花昇}}, who were advancing a series of movements aimed at abolishing and reforming the old system in Okinawa—including the {{ill|Somayama Issue|ja|杣山 (琉球王国)|lt=Somayama Issue|lt_ja=杣山問題}}—and suppressed the {{ill|Okinawa Club|ja|沖縄倶楽部}} organized by Sha using authoritarian measures. He served as an appointed governor for an unusually long 16-year term, earning the nickname "Ryukyu King." With the enactment of the {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture Land Readjustment Law|ja|沖縄県土地整理法}}, land readjustment projects were undertaken in the prefecture, and he also served as the president of the Okinawa Private Education Association.
=== Defectors from Qing === Thereafter, covert resistance by discontented samurai continued, and a succession of {{ill|Defectors from Qing|ja|脱清人}} emerged, either hoping for assistance from the {{ill|Qing Empire|ja|清国|lt=Qing Empire|lt_ja=清国}} or defecting to it on their own accord. In {{ill|1880|ja|1880年}} (Meiji 13), a proposal for the division of the {{ill|Sakishima Islands|ja|先島諸島}} (the Sakishima Islands Cession Plan) put forward by the Qing side led to a rapid increase in {{ill|Defectors|ja|亡命|lt=Defectors|lt_ja=亡命者}}{{要出典|date=2020年11月}}.

This group included not only King Shōtai and his relatives, but also Iē Chōei (the 12th head of the {{ill|Iē Goten|ja|伊江御殿}}, of the Mukō family) and {{ill|Nakijin Chōshiki|ja|今帰仁朝敷}} (the progenitor of the Nakijin Goten family).
